title: Double-Angle and Half-Angle Formulas
objectives: Derive double-angle formulas from the sum formulas
Apply double-angle formulas in simplification and computation
Derive half-angle formulas from double-angle formulas
Determine correct signs in half-angle formulas using quadrant analysis
Use power-reducing formulas to simplify expressions
Solve trigonometric equations using double-angle substitutions

content.title: Doubling and Halving
content.content:
In the parable of the talents (Matthew 25:14-30), the servants who 
invested their money saw it double. One received five talents and 
gained five more; another received two and gained two more. There's 
something mathematically elegant about doubling—and something 
correspondingly profound about halving. In this section, we explore 
how the trigonometric functions behave when angles are doubled or 
halved. These aren't simple operations: $\sin 2\theta \neq 2\sin\theta$. 
Instead, we discover intricate relationships that reveal deeper 
structure. Similarly, spiritual growth isn't always linear—sometimes 
a single insight doubles our understanding, while patient reflection 
helps us discern finer distinctions.

content.text:
Having developed the sum formulas, we can now derive formulas for special 
cases: what happens when both angles are the same ($\sin(\theta + \theta)$), 
and what happens when we work backward to find functions of half an angle.

The double-angle formulas arise naturally from the sum formulas when 
we set $\alpha = \beta = \theta$.

content.caption:
Visualizing the double angle on the unit circle. The point at 
angle $2\theta$ is related to the point at angle $\theta$ by the 
double-angle formulas.
content.alt:
Visualizing the double angle on the unit circle. The point at 
angle $2\theta$ is related to the point at angle $\theta$ by the 
double-angle formulas.

content.text:
The cosine double-angle formula can be rewritten using the Pythagorean 
identity. Since $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\cos 2\theta &= \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta \\
&= (1 - \sin^2\theta) - \sin^2\theta = 1 - 2\sin^2\theta \\
&= \cos^2\theta - (1 - \cos^2\theta) = 2\cos^2\theta - 1
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Common Error
content.content:
$\sin 2\theta \neq 2\sin\theta$. The factor of 2 "distributes" differently 
in trigonometry than in algebra. For example, $\sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, 
but $2\sin 30° = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 1$. These are not equal!

content.title: Computing double-angle values
content.problem:
If $\sin\theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant I, find 
$\sin 2\theta$, $\cos 2\theta$, and $\tan 2\theta$.
content.solution:
First, find $\cos\theta$. Since $\theta$ is in QI, cosine is positive:
$$
\cos\theta = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{9}{25}} = \sqrt{\frac{16}{25}} = \frac{4}{5}
$$

Now apply the double-angle formulas:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin 2\theta &= 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = 2 \cdot \frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{4}{5} = \frac{24}{25} \\[6pt]
\cos 2\theta &= \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \frac{16}{25} - \frac{9}{25} = \frac{7}{25} \\[6pt]
\tan 2\theta &= \frac{\sin 2\theta}{\cos 2\theta} = \frac{24/25}{7/25} = \frac{24}{7}
\end{aligned}$$

We can verify using the tangent formula directly. First, $\tan\theta = \frac{3/5}{4/5} = \frac{3}{4}$.
$$
\tan 2\theta = \frac{2\tan\theta}{1 - \tan^2\theta} = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{3}{4}}{1 - \frac{9}{16}} = \frac{\frac{3}{2}}{\frac{7}{16}} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{16}{7} = \frac{24}{7} \quad \checkmark
$$

content.title: Double angle in Quadrant III
content.problem:
If $\cos\theta = -\dfrac{5}{13}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant III, find 
$\sin 2\theta$ and $\cos 2\theta$. In what quadrant is $2\theta$?
content.solution:
Since $\theta$ is in QIII, sine is negative:
$$
\sin\theta = -\sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta} = -\sqrt{1 - \frac{25}{169}} = -\sqrt{\frac{144}{169}} = -\frac{12}{13}
$$

Applying the formulas:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin 2\theta &= 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = 2\left(-\frac{12}{13}\right)\left(-\frac{5}{13}\right) = \frac{120}{169} \\[6pt]
\cos 2\theta &= \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \frac{25}{169} - \frac{144}{169} = -\frac{119}{169}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\sin 2\theta > 0$ and $\cos 2\theta < 0$, the angle $2\theta$ is in Quadrant II.

**Checking the quadrant:** If $\theta$ is in QIII, then 
$\pi < \theta < \frac{3\pi}{2}$, so $2\pi < 2\theta < 3\pi$. 
This means $2\theta$ is between $2\pi$ and $3\pi$. After subtracting 
$2\pi$, we get an angle between $0$ and $\pi$, with cosine negative—that's QII.

content.title: Simplifying with double-angle formulas
content.problem: Simplify: $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$
content.solution:
This is exactly the form of $\cos 2\theta$:
$$
\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos 2\theta
$$

content.title: Recognizing double-angle patterns
content.problem: Simplify: $\dfrac{2\tan 3x}{1 - \tan^2 3x}$
content.solution:
This matches the tangent double-angle formula with $\theta = 3x$:
$$
\frac{2\tan 3x}{1 - \tan^2 3x} = \tan(2 \cdot 3x) = \tan 6x
$$

content.title: Deriving the Triple-Angle Formula for Sine
content.content:
We can derive $\sin 3\theta$ using the sum formula and double-angle formulas:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin 3\theta &= \sin(2\theta + \theta) \\
&= \sin 2\theta \cos\theta + \cos 2\theta \sin\theta
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute the double-angle formulas $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ 
and $\cos 2\theta = 1 - 2\sin^2\theta$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin 3\theta &= (2\sin\theta\cos\theta)\cos\theta + (1 - 2\sin^2\theta)\sin\theta \\
&= 2\sin\theta\cos^2\theta + \sin\theta - 2\sin^3\theta
\end{aligned}$$

Now replace $\cos^2\theta = 1 - \sin^2\theta$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin 3\theta &= 2\sin\theta(1 - \sin^2\theta) + \sin\theta - 2\sin^3\theta \\
&= 2\sin\theta - 2\sin^3\theta + \sin\theta - 2\sin^3\theta \\
&= 3\sin\theta - 4\sin^3\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Triple-Angle Formulas:**
$$
\boxed{\sin 3\theta = 3\sin\theta - 4\sin^3\theta}
$$
$$
\boxed{\cos 3\theta = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta}
$$

The cosine triple-angle formula is derived similarly (try it as an exercise!).

**Application:** These formulas are used to solve cubic equations and 
to construct regular polygons with compass and straightedge.

content.text:
Rearranging the cosine double-angle formulas gives us formulas that 
reduce powers of sine and cosine:

content.title: Using power-reducing formulas
content.problem: Rewrite $\cos^4\theta$ without powers greater than 1.

content.text:
The power-reducing formulas, with a change of variable, become 
half-angle formulas. If we let $\theta = \frac{\alpha}{2}$, then 
$2\theta = \alpha$, and:
$$
\sin^2\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{1 - \cos\alpha}{2}
$$
The $\pm$ sign is determined by the quadrant in which $\frac{\alpha}{2}$ lies.

content.title: Half-Angle Formulas
content.content:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin\frac{\theta}{2} &= \pm\sqrt{\frac{1 - \cos\theta}{2}} \\[6pt]
\cos\frac{\theta}{2} &= \pm\sqrt{\frac{1 + \cos\theta}{2}} \\[6pt]
\tan\frac{\theta}{2} &= \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \frac{\sin\theta}{1 + \cos\theta}
\end{aligned}$$
The sign of the sine and cosine half-angle formulas depends on the 
quadrant in which $\frac{\theta}{2}$ lies.

content.caption:
Visualizing the half angle. Given a point at angle $\theta$, the 
half-angle formulas tell us the coordinates of the point at angle $\frac{\theta}{2}$.
content.alt:
Visualizing the half angle. Given a point at angle $\theta$, the 
half-angle formulas tell us the coordinates of the point at angle $\frac{\theta}{2}$.

content.title: Determining the Sign
content.content:
When using the half-angle formulas for sine and cosine, you must 
determine the correct sign:

1. Identify the interval containing $\theta$.

2. Divide by 2 to find the interval containing $\frac{\theta}{2}$.

3. Determine which quadrant $\frac{\theta}{2}$ is in.

4. Use ASTC to determine the sign of sine or cosine in that quadrant.

content.title: Finding $\cos 22.5°$
content.problem: Find the exact value of $\cos 22.5°$.
content.solution:
We note that $22.5° = \frac{45°}{2}$, so we use the half-angle formula 
with $\theta = 45°$.

Since $22.5°$ is in Quadrant I, cosine is positive, so we take the 
positive square root:
$$\begin{aligned}
\cos 22.5° &= \cos\frac{45°}{2} = +\sqrt{\frac{1 + \cos 45°}{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1 + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{\frac{2 + \sqrt{2}}{2}}{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 + \sqrt{2}}{4}} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2}}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Finding $\sin 15°$
content.problem: Find the exact value of $\sin 15°$ using the half-angle formula.
content.solution:
Since $15° = \frac{30°}{2}$ and $15°$ is in QI (sine positive):
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin 15° &= +\sqrt{\frac{1 - \cos 30°}{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1 - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{\frac{2 - \sqrt{3}}{2}}{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 - \sqrt{3}}{4}} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{2 - \sqrt{3}}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Finding $\tan\frac{\pi}{8}$
content.problem: Find the exact value of $\tan\dfrac{\pi}{8}$.

content.title: Half-angle with sign determination
content.problem:
If $\cos\theta = -\dfrac{4}{5}$ and $\pi < \theta < \frac{3\pi}{2}$, 
find $\sin\dfrac{\theta}{2}$ and $\cos\dfrac{\theta}{2}$.

content.text: Verifying Identities with Double and Half Angles

content.title: Simplifying with double-angle
content.problem: Simplify: $\dfrac{1 - \cos 2x}{\sin 2x}$
content.solution:
We use the identities $1 - \cos 2x = 2\sin^2 x$ and $\sin 2x = 2\sin x\cos x$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1 - \cos 2x}{\sin 2x} &= \frac{2\sin^2 x}{2\sin x\cos x} \\
&= \frac{\sin x}{\cos x} \\
&= \tan x
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: A double-angle identity
content.problem: Verify: $\dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{1 + \cos 2\theta} = \tan\theta$

content.title: Multiple Representations
content.content:
The cosine double-angle formula has three equivalent forms: 
$\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$, $2\cos^2\theta - 1$, and $1 - 2\sin^2\theta$. 
Each says the same thing differently. This multiplicity of representations 
mirrors how Scripture often presents the same truth from different angles. 
The four Gospels tell one story through four perspectives. A skilled 
mathematician (or theologian) learns when each form is most useful.

content.text:
Double-angle formulas often help us solve trigonometric equations by 
reducing them to simpler forms.

content.title: Solving with double-angle substitution
content.problem: Find all solutions of $\cos 2x + \cos x = 0$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$.
content.solution:
First, use the double-angle formula $\cos 2x = 2\cos^2 x - 1$ to write 
everything in terms of $\cos x$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\cos 2x + \cos x &= 0 \\
(2\cos^2 x - 1) + \cos x &= 0 \\
2\cos^2 x + \cos x - 1 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

This is a quadratic in $\cos x$. Let $u = \cos x$:
$$
2u^2 + u - 1 = 0 \implies (2u - 1)(u + 1) = 0
$$

So $u = \frac{1}{2}$ or $u = -1$, meaning $\cos x = \frac{1}{2}$ or $\cos x = -1$.

**Case 1:** $\cos x = \frac{1}{2}$ gives $x = \frac{\pi}{3}$ or $x = \frac{5\pi}{3}$

**Case 2:** $\cos x = -1$ gives $x = \pi$

The solutions in $[0, 2\pi)$ are $x = \dfrac{\pi}{3}, \pi, \dfrac{5\pi}{3}$.

content.title: Equation with sin 2x
content.problem: Solve $\sin 2x = \cos x$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi)$.
content.solution:
Use $\sin 2x = 2\sin x\cos x$:
$$\begin{aligned}
2\sin x\cos x &= \cos x \\
2\sin x\cos x - \cos x &= 0 \\
\cos x(2\sin x - 1) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

So $\cos x = 0$ or $\sin x = \frac{1}{2}$.

**Case 1:** $\cos x = 0$ gives $x = \frac{\pi}{2}$ or $x = \frac{3\pi}{2}$

**Case 2:** $\sin x = \frac{1}{2}$ gives $x = \frac{\pi}{6}$ or $x = \frac{5\pi}{6}$

The solutions are $x = \dfrac{\pi}{6}, \dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{5\pi}{6}, \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$.

content.title: Double-Angle and Half-Angle Formulas Reference

content.problem:
Fill in the blank.

1. The formula $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ is called the formula.

2. The cosine double-angle formula can be written in equivalent forms.

3. The formulas $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$ and $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$ are called the formulas.

Fill in the blank.

1. In the half-angle formula $\sin\frac{\theta}{2} = \pm\sqrt{\frac{1-\cos\theta}{2}}$, 
 the sign is determined by .

2. The Weierstrass substitution $t = \tan\frac{\theta}{2}$ expresses 
 $\sin\theta$ as .

3. The tangent half-angle formula $\tan\frac{\theta}{2} = \frac{1-\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}$ 
 avoids $\pm$ because .

True or False? Justify your answer.

1. $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta$

2. $\cos 2\theta$ can be written as $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$, $2\cos^2\theta - 1$, or $1 - 2\sin^2\theta$.

3. $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$

4. If $\cos\theta > 0$, then $\cos 2\theta > 0$.
Why are there three forms of the double-angle formula for cosine but only one for sine?
Explain why $\sin 2\theta \neq 2\sin\theta$ using a specific example.
content.answer: (a) False (b) True (c) True (d) True (e) False; depends on quadrant of $\theta/2$

content.problem: In the half-angle formula, how do you determine whether to use $+$ or $-$? Illustrate with $\sin 105°$.
Can $\cos 2\theta$ be negative when $\cos\theta$ is positive? Give an example or explain why not.
content.answer:
Three forms of $\cos 2\theta$: useful depending on which function appears in the problem; 
power-reducing comes from solving for $\sin^2\theta$ or $\cos^2\theta$.

content.problem:
Simplify using double-angle formulas to find the exact value.

1. $2\sin 15°\cos 15°$

2. $\cos^2 22.5° - \sin^2 22.5°$

3. $2\cos^2 75° - 1$

4. $1 - 2\sin^2 15°$

5. $\dfrac{2\tan 22.5°}{1 - \tan^2 22.5°}$

6. $2\sin\dfrac{\pi}{8}\cos\dfrac{\pi}{8}$

Simplify to a single trigonometric function.

1. $2\sin 3x\cos 3x$

2. $\cos^2 5\theta - \sin^2 5\theta$

3. $1 - 2\sin^2 4t$

4. $2\cos^2\dfrac{x}{2} - 1$

5. $\dfrac{2\tan 2\alpha}{1 - \tan^2 2\alpha}$

6. $\cos^2 2x - \sin^2 2x$
content.answer: $\theta/2$ could be in a different quadrant than $\theta$; must determine quadrant of $\theta/2$ first.

content.problem:
Simplify each expression.

1. $\dfrac{\sin 2x}{\cos x}$

2. $\dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2\cos\theta}$

3. $\dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$

4. $\dfrac{1 + \cos 2\alpha}{2}$

If $\sin\theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant I, find:

1. $\sin 2\theta$

2. $\cos 2\theta$

3. $\tan 2\theta$

4. Quadrant of $2\theta$
content.answer:
$\theta$ in QII means $\frac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi$, so $\frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{\theta}{2} < \frac{\pi}{2}$ (QI). 
Thus both $\sin\frac{\theta}{2}$ and $\cos\frac{\theta}{2}$ are positive.

1. $\sin 2\theta = \dfrac{2\tan\theta}{1 + \tan^2\theta}$

2. $\cos 2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \tan^2\theta}{1 + \tan^2\theta}$

The range of a projectile launched at angle $\theta$ with initial velocity $v_0$ is 
$R = \dfrac{v_0^2\sin 2\theta}{g}$.

1. At what angle(s) is the range maximized?

2. Show that launching at $\theta$ and at $90° - \theta$ gives the same range.

3. If $v_0 = 80$ ft/s and $g = 32$ ft/s², find $\theta$ for a range of 150 ft.

The intensity of light through two polarizing filters at angle $\theta$ is 
$I = I_0\cos^2\theta$. Use a power-reducing formula to show this equals 
$\dfrac{I_0}{2}(1 + \cos 2\theta)$.

In calculus, integrals of $\sin^2 x$ and $\cos^2 x$ are computed using power-reducing formulas.

1. Show that $\sin^2 x = \dfrac{1}{2}(1 - \cos 2x)$.

2. Show that $\cos^2 x = \dfrac{1}{2}(1 + \cos 2x)$.

3. Use these to show that $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$.

The area of a triangle with sides $a$ and $b$ and included angle $\theta$ is 
$A = \frac{1}{2}ab\sin\theta$. For an isoceles triangle with two equal sides 
of length $s$ and apex angle $2\theta$, show that the area is $A = s^2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{1}{2}s^2\sin 2\theta$.

When an airplane travels faster than the speed of sound, the sound waves form 
a cone behind the airplane. The Mach number $M$ (ratio of plane speed to sound speed) 
is related to the apex half-angle $\theta$ of the cone by $\sin\dfrac{\theta}{2} = \dfrac{1}{M}$.

1. Use a half-angle formula to rewrite the equation in terms of $\cos\theta$.

2. Find the apex angle $\theta$ for a Mach 2 aircraft.

3. Find the apex angle $\theta$ for a Mach 3 aircraft.

4. The speed of sound is approximately 761 mph. What is the speed of a Mach 2.5 aircraft?
Graph $y = \sin^2 x$ and $y = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2x}{2}$ on the same axes. What do you observe? Explain using power-reducing formulas.
Graph $y = \cos 2x$, $y = 2\cos^2 x - 1$, and $y = 1 - 2\sin^2 x$ together. What do you observe?
Use a graphing utility to verify that $\cos 22.5° = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + \cos 45°}{2}}$ by computing both sides numerically.

Describe and correct the error.

Finding $\cos 2\theta$ if $\sin\theta = \frac{3}{5}$:
> "$\cos 2\theta = 2\cos^2\theta - 1 = 2\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^2 - 1 = \frac{32}{25} - 1 = \frac{7}{25}$"

(What assumption was made? Is this always valid?)

Describe and correct the error.

Simplifying $\cos 4\theta$:
> "$\cos 4\theta = \cos(2 \cdot 2\theta) = 2\cos 2\theta$" ✗

Describe and correct the error.

Finding $\sin\frac{\theta}{2}$ when $\cos\theta = -\frac{3}{5}$ and $\theta$ is in QII:
> "Since $\theta$ is in QII, $\frac{\theta}{2}$ is in QI, so $\sin\frac{\theta}{2} = +\sqrt{\frac{1-(-3/5)}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{5}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$"

(Is the reasoning correct? Check the quadrant determination.)
Verify: $\tan\dfrac{\theta}{2} = \dfrac{1 - \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{1 + \cos\theta}$
Show that $\sin 3\theta = 3\sin\theta - 4\sin^3\theta$ using $\sin 3\theta = \sin(2\theta + \theta)$.
Derive the formula $\cos 3\theta = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta$.
Derive a formula for $\tan 3\theta$ in terms of $\tan\theta$.
Prove: $\sin 4\theta = 4\sin\theta\cos\theta\cos 2\theta$
Prove: $\cos 4\theta = 8\cos^4\theta - 8\cos^2\theta + 1$
Express $\sin 5\theta$ in terms of powers of $\sin\theta$.

Derive the formula $\sin^3\theta = \dfrac{3\sin\theta - \sin 3\theta}{4}$.

 Derive the half-angle formula for sine starting from $\cos 2\alpha = 1 - 2\sin^2\alpha$. Let $\alpha = \frac{\theta}{2}$ and solve for $\sin\frac{\theta}{2}$.

 Show that the two forms of the tangent half-angle formula are equivalent:
$$
\frac{1 - \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \frac{\sin\theta}{1 + \cos\theta}
$$

 The Weierstrass substitution $t = \tan\frac{\theta}{2}$ is useful in calculus. Show that:

1. $\sin\theta = \dfrac{2t}{1 + t^2}$

2. $\cos\theta = \dfrac{1 - t^2}{1 + t^2}$

3. $\tan\theta = \dfrac{2t}{1 - t^2}$

 Derive the power-reducing formula $\tan^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{1 + \cos 2\theta}$ from the formulas for $\sin^2\theta$ and $\cos^2\theta$.
